checkpoints.label: Lesson 1 — Present tense verbs
checkpoints.summary: Third-person sentences, ego/tu, conjugation patterns, infinitives, discit/et, magistra/docet, proverb
checkpoints.vocabulary: poeta, discipula, scribo/scribis/scribit, studeo/studes/studet, lego/legis/legit, dormio/dormis/dormit, agricola, laboro/laboras/laborat, ego, tu, discit, et, magistra, doceo/doces/docet, qui
checkpoints.grammar: Present indicative; 1st/2nd/3rd person singular; verb endings -o / -s / -t; infinitives in -are/-ere; coordinated verbs with et

checkpoints.label: Accusative objects
checkpoints.summary: Word-order interlude, then accusative -m on objects; epistulam, pro-drop, video, bibit
checkpoints.vocabulary: epistula, magistram, discipulam, poetam, aquam, (review of lesson 1 verbs)
checkpoints.grammar: Accusative case marks direct object; flexible word order; subject omitted when clear; video/videt/vides; bibit/bibere

checkpoints.label: Genitive & adjectives
checkpoints.summary: Possessive -ae; adjective agreement (longa/magna/pulchra); sed, non, nemo
checkpoints.vocabulary: poetae, agricolae, magistrai, longa, magna, pulchra, sed, non, nemo, bene
checkpoints.grammar: Genitive (possessive) case; adjective endings match noun case/number/gender; negation with non; nemo

checkpoints.label: Story — the farmer's letter
checkpoints.summary: First Latin story; infinitive with docet; masculine/feminine noun pairs
checkpoints.vocabulary: quod, bene (adverb), discipulus, amicus/amica, habeo/habes/habet, (story vocabulary)
checkpoints.grammar: Infinitive complements (scribere docet); gender pairs (-a/-us); story reading

checkpoints.label: Second declension & esse
checkpoints.summary: 1st vs 2nd declension; liber/librum, gladius; first/second declension tables
checkpoints.vocabulary: discipulus, amicus, liber, gladius, librum, gladium, discipuli, amici
checkpoints.grammar: First declension (mostly feminine -a); second declension masculine (-us/-um/-i); nominative vs accusative in 2nd declension

checkpoints.label: Esse, possession & Europa story (part 1)
checkpoints.summary: Sum/es/est; mea/meus; posse; desiderare; puella; Jupiter story begins
checkpoints.vocabulary: est, sum, es, mea, meus, magnus/magna, potest, pos, desidero/desiderat, puella, puellae, deus, terra, habitat, spectat, Europa, timida, taurus, se
checkpoints.grammar: Irregular verb esse; predicate adjectives; posse + infinitive; story narrative

checkpoints.label: Ablative — location & motion
checkpoints.summary: In + ablative for location; in/ad + accusative for motion into; vinum, silva
checkpoints.vocabulary: vinum, silva, siluam, ambulo/ambulas, dea, ad, Olympo/Olympum, Olympi
checkpoints.grammar: Ablative of place where (in + abl); accusative of place to which (in/ad + acc); neuter nouns (-um); prepositions in and ad

checkpoints.label: Declension review & plurals
checkpoints.summary: Noun tables; plural forms; insula story; dative/locative preview; dare
checkpoints.vocabulary: insula, insulae, amici, discipuli, discunt, legunt, omnis, Roma, Italia, Europa, viam/viae, ducit, habito, dare, das, damus, magister, tunicam
checkpoints.grammar: Plural nominative/accusative; locative (Romae); dative (indirect object); verb dare; plural verb endings

checkpoints.label: Third declension & dative practice
checkpoints.summary: Miles, felis, rex; third-declension patterns; dative with dare
checkpoints.vocabulary: miles, felis, rex, civis, corpus, (third-declension nouns from lesson)
checkpoints.grammar: Third declension nouns; genitive stem; dative case practice; comparative declension review

checkpoints.label: Lord's Prayer — imperative & subjunctive
checkpoints.summary: Pater noster walkthrough; subjunctive and imperative preview
checkpoints.vocabulary: pater, caelum, nomen, regnum, voluntas, panis, peccata, debitor, tentatio, libera
checkpoints.grammar: Imperative mood; subjunctive preview; prayer as capstone reading

What does *dormire* mean?
*scribit* = 'writes'. The infinitive **'to write'** is scrib__ (type the ending):
Arrange these words to say: "I teach and you teach."
Arrange these words to say: "The teacher teaches and the student learns."
The proverb *qui docet discit* means:
Every sentence has a **verb** — the action word. In *'The girl writes a letter'*, which word is the verb?
The **subject** is who (or what) does the action. In *'The girl writes a letter'*, what is the subject?
The **object** is who (or what) the action is done to. In *'The girl writes a letter'*, what is the object?
In English: *'The dog bites the man.'* Who does the biting (the **subject**)?
Still *'The dog bites the man.'* Who gets bitten (the **object**)?
Now the words are reordered: *'The man bites the dog.'* Who does the biting now?
In English, what told you 'the dog' was the **subject** in 'The dog bites the man' but the **object** in 'The man bites the dog'?

options.text: its position — which side of the verb it sits on (word order)

questions.prompt: English shows subject vs object by **word order**. Latin does it a different way — it changes the word's …
In Latin, adding **-m** to a noun (epistula → epistula**m**) marks it as the …

options.text: object — the thing the action is done to

questions.prompt: Each noun below is either a subject or an object. Select ALL the ones that are **objects** (having the action done to them).
*aqua* = 'water'. To make it the **object** ('… the water'), add one letter: aqua__
*magistra* (the teacher, as subject). Type the **object** form (the teacher, being taught/seen):
Listen to both clips. Which one is the **object** form — 'the teacher' being acted upon?
Sentence: **magistra discipulam docet.** (*magistra* ends in -a = subject; *discipulam* ends in -am = object.) Who is doing the teaching?
Now the endings swap: **discipula magistram docet.** Who is doing the teaching *this* time?
*magistra discipulam docet* and *discipula magistram docet* use almost the same words. What decides **who teaches whom**?
Because the *endings* carry the meaning, Latin can move words around. Do **poeta epistulam scribit** and **epistulam poeta scribit** mean the same thing?

options.text: Yes — poeta is still the writer, epistulam still the letter
No — moving the words swaps who does what
